content: Hey Saturday, I'm seriously thinking about getting a dog. I've been living alone for a couple years now and feel like it's time. I work from home mostly, so I have the time. What should I be thinking about before I actually commit to this?

That's exciting! Working from home is definitely a huge advantage for dog ownership. Before you commit, think through a few key things: first, your lifestyle and activity level — do you want a high-energy dog that needs 2+ hours of exercise daily, or something more chill? Second, your living space and whether you have a yard. Third, the financial commitment — vet bills, food, training, emergencies can easily run $1,500-3,000+ per year. Fourth, how long you're willing to be tied down — dogs live 10-15+ years. And finally, whether you're ready for the training and behavioral work, especially in the first year. What's your current setup like, and what kind of energy level appeals to you?

I've got a decent-sized apartment in the city with no yard, but there's a big park about two blocks away. I'm pretty active — I run and hike on weekends — so I could definitely handle an energetic dog. Money-wise, I'm stable enough that I'm not worried about affording it. I just want to make sure I pick a breed that actually fits my situation.

Nice, that's a solid setup. The proximity to the park is really helpful. Given that you're active and have urban living with no yard, you have some good options. High-energy breeds like Border Collies, Australian Shepherds, or Vizslas could work well since you can give them serious exercise. But here's the thing — many of those breeds also need mental stimulation and can develop anxiety or destructive habits without it. Medium-energy dogs like Brittanys, Beagles, or mixed breeds often strike a good balance. Honestly, I'd also suggest not fixating too hard on breed and considering shelter dogs — mixed breeds often have fewer genetic health issues and are incredibly grateful. What's your tolerance for shedding? That factors into the decision too, especially in an apartment.

I haven't thought much about shedding, but I guess it's worth considering since I'm in an apartment. I'm not obsessive about cleaning, but I don't want hair everywhere. Are there specific low-shedding breeds, or is that more of a myth? Also, do you think I should start looking at shelters or breeders? I've heard shelters have amazing dogs but also maybe more behavioral issues?

Low-shedding breeds definitely exist — Poodles, Doodle mixes, Portuguese Water Dogs, Schnauzers, and Bichons are genuinely low-shedding. But here's the reality: low-shedding dogs often need regular grooming (every 6-8 weeks, $50-100+ per session) to keep their coats healthy. That's an ongoing cost and time commitment. As for shelters vs. breeders, I'd honestly start with shelters first. Yes, some shelter dogs have past trauma or behavioral issues, but many are surrendered for life reasons — owner relocated, divorce, job change — not because they're problem dogs. Plus, adoption fees are usually $150-300 versus $1,000-3,000+ for a breeder puppy. That said, puppies from breeders let you know their health history and personality from the start, whereas shelter dogs are sometimes mystery boxes. Maybe compromise: look at breed-specific rescues (like Border Collie rescues) that have adult dogs with known histories?

I really like the idea of a breed-specific rescue. Do you know how to find those, or is it just Google searching? And should I be prepared with supplies before I actually bring a dog home, or can I figure that out after?

Google works, but also check Petfinder.com and Rescue Me (rescueme.org) — they have networks of breed-specific rescues organized by location. You can filter by breed and see available dogs. Some breeds have huge rescue communities, so your options might be pretty good. As for supplies, absolutely prepare beforehand — don't wait until you bring the dog home. You'll want: a quality collar, leash, and harness; food and water bowls; a crate for training and safety (very important); a bed or two; toys for play and mental enrichment; grooming supplies; and obviously food. If you're getting an adult dog from a rescue, they'll usually tell you what food they've been eating — switching too fast causes digestive issues. Budget maybe $300-500 for initial setup, more if you're getting higher-end stuff. Setting up before pickup day makes the transition way smoother and less stressful for both of you.

Good point about the food transition. How long does that usually take? And I'm wondering what the typical process is for adopting from a shelter or rescue — do they just let you take a dog home, or is there an application and stuff?

Food transitions usually take 7-10 days. You gradually mix more of the new food in with the old food each day until it's 100% new food. Going faster can cause diarrhea or upset stomach. As for the adoption process, it varies but most rescues are pretty thorough. Expect an application asking about your living situation, work schedule, experience with dogs, vet references if you've had dogs before, and sometimes a home visit. They want to make sure it's actually a good fit and not just someone impulsively getting a dog. It can take a few days to a couple weeks to get approved. Once approved, there's usually an adoption fee ($150-400 range), and they'll give you the dog's medical history, vaccinations, and maybe microchip info. Some rescues have trial periods too, where you can return the dog within a few weeks if it's not working out. That's actually really helpful because sometimes the home situation is different than expected.

A trial period sounds great actually — takes some of the pressure off. So once I adopt, what's the first thing I should focus on? Like training, settling them in, or just giving them time to decompress?

Good question, and honestly it's a combination. The first week is about decompression and building trust — your dog is in a new environment with a stranger, so they're stressed even if they seem fine. Stick to a consistent routine for sleeping, eating, and potty breaks. Don't overwhelm them with visitors or too much activity. That said, start some basic structure immediately: take them outside frequently for bathroom breaks (every 2-3 hours for the first week), establish a sleep spot (ideally the crate), and set feeding times. This actually helps them feel secure because they know what to expect. Training — sit, stay, basic obedience — can wait a week or two. Focus on bonding and establishing that you're a safe person. If the dog was a shelter dog with unknown background, watching their behavior will help you understand what they need. Some need gradual confidence building, others are ready to go immediately. Also, schedule a vet checkup within the first week just to make sure there are no hidden health issues.

That makes sense. I'm starting to feel more ready for this. Before I actually start looking at rescues, is there anything else I'm missing? Like, should I take time off work when I first bring the dog home, or is that overthinking it?

Taking time off is actually smart, not overthinking. At least 3-5 days off work would be ideal for the first week — you need to establish those bathroom breaks, help them adjust, and be there if they're anxious. Since you work from home though, you have an advantage. You don't need a whole week off like someone in an office would, but at least a few days where you're not doing intensive work calls would be really helpful. Here's something else to think about: crate training and potty training go hand-in-hand, especially if it's an adult dog from a shelter. Some are already trained, some aren't. Setting expectations about accidents is important — don't get frustrated, it's not personal. Also, consider whether you want to hire a dog walker or trainer down the line, especially while you're figuring out the routine. And honestly, just mentally prepare for the fact that the first month is hard. It's rewarding but exhausting. Sleep disruption, unexpected behavioral stuff, vet costs you didn't anticipate. It's worth it, but go in with eyes open.
You're being real about this which I appreciate. So when you say unexpected vet costs, what kind of stuff are you talking about? Should I set aside emergency money, or is that paranoid?

Definitely not paranoid. Absolutely have an emergency fund set aside — aim for at least $1,000-2,000. Here's what comes up: shelter dogs sometimes have parasites (fleas, worms, intestinal stuff) that need treatment ($100-300). Dental issues are super common and can cost $300-2,000 if they need cleaning or extractions. Ear infections, skin allergies, joint problems pop up unexpectedly and each visit plus diagnostics can be $200-500+. Then there's just annual stuff: vaccines, heartworm tests, nail trims. A routine vet visit is $100-200. If something serious happens — injury, bloat, poisoning — you could easily be looking at $2,000-5,000+ for emergency care. Some people get pet insurance, which costs $30-50/month depending on coverage, and that's honestly not a bad idea given you're a single person with the dog as your responsibility. It protects against catastrophic costs. So yeah, set aside emergency money and consider insurance as a safety net.
Pet insurance is interesting. I haven't heard much about it. Is there a catch, or are some companies actually good? And how does it work — do you pay upfront and get reimbursed?

Pet insurance is legit but yeah, there are catches to understand. How it works: you pay a monthly premium ($30-100+ depending on age and breed), then when you have a vet bill, you either pay upfront and submit for reimbursement, or some clinics bill directly. Most plans reimburse 70-90% of costs after a deductible (usually $100-500 per year). The catch is that pre-existing conditions aren't covered, and different plans have different limits on payouts per incident or per year. Also, premiums increase as your dog ages. Popular companies include Pawlicy Advisor, Fetch, and ASPCA Pet Insurance — they have different review ratings, so research is important. The key is buying before anything goes wrong, because once your dog has a condition, that becomes pre-existing and won't be covered. For a young healthy dog from a rescue, it might be worth it just for peace of mind. Some people skip it if they have good savings already, but I think for someone just starting out with a dog, insurance removes a lot of stress about unexpected costs.

Okay, I think insurance makes sense. Now I'm actually ready to start looking. Should I pick a specific breed first, or just browse shelters and see if something clicks? Like, should I have a breed list or go in open-minded?

I'd say do both: have a general framework but stay open-minded. Given your lifestyle — apartment, active, limited yard — make a list of 3-5 breeds that fit: maybe Border Collie, Australian Shepherd, mixed herding breeds, maybe a Pointer breed if you like hunting dogs. Then check breed-specific rescues for those first. BUT — and this is important — if you see a dog that's not exactly your breed but the personality and energy level click, don't dismiss it. Some of the best dogs are mixed breeds or breeds you didn't plan for. Go visit shelters and spend time with dogs. See how they react to you, how they respond to commands, whether they seem anxious or confident. The dog's personality matters way more than the breed. Also look at the rescue's description — they usually note if the dog is good with cats, kids, other dogs, or has any quirks. Pay attention to that stuff because it directly affects whether the dog will be happy in your situation.

That's helpful. I'm going to start looking at breed-specific rescues this week. One question though — when I visit a dog at a rescue or shelter, what should I actually be looking for or testing? Like, is there a way to assess if a dog is right for me before committing?

Absolutely, and most good rescues will facilitate this. Ask the staff what they know about the dog's behavior: energy level, how they walk on leash, any training already done, how they react to new people, other dogs, or cats if relevant. Then ask if you can spend time with the dog — walk them, play with them, see how they respond to you. Watch for a few things: Do they make eye contact and seem engaged with you, or are they anxious and distant? Can they take treats gently from your hand, or are they food-aggressive? How do they walk on leash — pulling constantly, or somewhat responsive to direction? Do they seem to have any fear behaviors — cowering, excessive barking, aggression? Honestly, some rescues will do a trial period, so you could even do that, which takes pressure off making a perfect assessment. Also ask about their return policy explicitly — like if you adopt and it's not working in week two, can you bring the dog back? Good rescues are fine with this. The staff usually knows these dogs well, so listen to their honest take. If they seem hesitant about a match, respect that.

That's really thorough advice. I appreciate it. One last thing before I dive in — should I talk to my landlord first about having a dog, or is that unnecessary since most places allow them with a fee?

Absolutely talk to your landlord first — do not skip this step. Every lease is different, and some places don't allow dogs at all, or have breed restrictions, size limits, or breed bans (which is whole other debate). You could adopt a dog and then get told you have to give it up, which would be devastating. Check your lease, then contact your landlord or property management proactively. Be honest about getting a dog, ask about any fees or deposits required. Most landlords charge $200-500 pet deposit plus maybe $25-50/month extra pet rent, but it varies wildly. Some places have no restrictions at all. Get the approval in writing if you can. It's a quick conversation that saves you from a disaster later. Plus, being proactive and asking first puts you in good standing with your landlord — they're more likely to be cool about it if you approach it responsibly rather than them finding out you secretly got a dog.

Good call, I definitely don't want that surprise conversation. Okay, I think I've got a solid plan now. I'm going to check my lease, talk to my landlord, start looking at breed-specific rescues this week, and gather supplies. One thing I'm suddenly worried about — what if I'm not actually ready for this? What if I get a dog and realize I made a mistake?

That's a totally valid worry, and honestly? Most people who get a dog feel that panic at some point, usually within the first two weeks. It's normal. But here's the thing — you've done way more thinking about this than most people do. You're working from home, financially stable, active, and you're genuinely preparing. That puts you ahead of the game. That said, those trial periods I mentioned exist partly because sometimes the reality doesn't match the plan, and that's okay. If within a few weeks you realize it's genuinely not working, you can return the dog to the rescue and they'll find it another home. That's not a failure — that's being responsible. But I suspect once you actually have a dog and get past that initial hard month, you'll be surprised how quickly that panic goes away and how rewarding it becomes. The fact that you're worried about being ready tells me you probably are. Go start looking, visit some dogs, and see if it clicks. You don't have to commit fully until you actually meet a dog that feels right.
Alright, I feel a lot better about this. I'm going to start the research phase this week. I'll let you know how it goes when I visit some rescues. Hopefully I'll have some good stories soon!

That's awesome, I'm excited for you! Go check out those rescues and take your time finding the right fit. One small thing before you go — maybe make a simple checklist of what you're looking for (energy level, size, any specific breed preferences) and bring it with you to rescues so you stay focused while you're there. Also, don't feel pressured to decide the first day — good rescues won't push you, and it's totally fine to go back and visit the same dog multiple times if you're on the fence. And feel free to come back and tell me what you find, ask questions about specific dogs, or get advice on supplies once you have a dog in mind. The hard part is behind you — you've done the thinking, now it's just the fun exploration part. Good luck, and I hope you find your match!
Will do! Quick question before I go though — any tips for the actual rescue visit? Like should I bring anything, dress a certain way, or just show up and talk to staff?

Good questions! Practically speaking: wear comfortable clothes and shoes you don't mind getting dirty, because you'll probably be playing with dogs or walking them outside. Bring a small notebook to jot down dog names and notes about ones you connect with — you'll probably see multiple dogs and they blur together. Don't bring too many people (just go yourself ideally), because dogs react differently with crowds and the staff can give you more focused attention. Bring your phone to take photos of dogs you like, which helps you remember them later. As for attitude, just be genuine and ask lots of questions. Staff appreciate people who are seriously thinking this through, not just impulse browsing. They'll warm up to you if they sense you're being thoughtful. Don't pretend to be okay with something you're not — if a dog seems way more energetic than you want, be honest about it. The rescue wants the best match too, so they'll appreciate your honesty. Show up with excitement but also genuine curiosity about what each dog's personality is like. You're interviewing them as much as they're evaluating you!

Perfect, I feel ready. I'm going to hit up a couple rescues this Saturday. I'll probably look at breed-specific ones first since we talked about those being good options. Anything else I should keep in mind going in?

Just one more thing — manage your expectations a bit. You might not find the perfect dog this week, and that's totally fine. Sometimes it takes visiting multiple rescues over a few weeks before something clicks. Don't feel like you have to commit just because a dog is cute or available. That said, when you find the right dog, you'll probably feel it — there's usually a moment where you just know. Also, the rescue will have you fill out an application if you're seriously interested, and that takes a few days to process, so don't expect to take a dog home same-day. This gives you time to sit with your choice and make sure. Most importantly, go enjoy the process and meet some dogs. You're at the fun part now. Saturday visits sound perfect — you'll have time to visit multiple places and not feel rushed. Take your time, ask questions, pet some dogs, and see how it feels. Feel free to message me after your visits with updates or questions about specific dogs you meet!

I will definitely reach out after Saturday. I'm going to check out the Border Collie rescue and maybe a general shelter too to see the range. I have a really good feeling about this whole thing now, so thanks for walking me through everything. You made it feel way less overwhelming.

That's awesome, and I'm glad I could help make it feel more manageable. Border Collie rescues are great — those dogs are incredible once you understand their energy needs. And yes, check out a general shelter too because sometimes you find unexpected gems. The combination of breed-specific and general shelter browsing usually gives you the best sense of what's available in your area. You've got a solid plan, you're thinking about this the right way, and your future dog is lucky that they're getting someone this thoughtful. Go have fun at the rescues on Saturday, enjoy meeting some dogs, and definitely come back and tell me about it. Whether you find your dog this week or it takes a few visits, I'm here to help you think through specific dogs, answer questions about supplies, whatever you need. Now go prepare that apartment and landlord conversation, and enjoy the next phase of this adventure!
One more thing popped into my head — when I visit the rescue, should I ask about the dog's history? Like, will they have info about where the dog came from or why it was surrendered?

Great question, and yes, absolutely ask. Most rescues have some background on where the dog came from — owner surrender, stray found on the street, picked up by animal control, etc. Some even have the original owner's reason if it was a surrender (job loss, moving, divorce, medical issues, etc.). This information is actually really valuable because it tells you about potential trauma or behavioral patterns. A dog that was abused will need different handling than one that was just surrendered because the owner's life circumstances changed. A stray might have been on the street for months and need patience with house training. The rescue staff can also tell you if the dog has been through behavioral assessment, so you know what you're getting. That said, sometimes shelters don't have complete history — strays especially are mysteries. In those cases, staff observations matter most. They've seen how the dog behaves in the shelter, how it walks, plays, reacts to people. That real-time behavioral info is honestly more useful than a story that might be incomplete anyway. So definitely ask, but also weight the staff's firsthand observations heavily.

Got it. I think asking about history really helps me understand the dog better and what kind of patience or support they might need. This is good intel. Alright, I think I'm actually ready now. Going to make that landlord call tomorrow and prepare the apartment this week. Really excited!

Perfect timeline! Get that landlord approval done early so it's one less thing to worry about. This week prep means you'll be ready to move fast if you find your dog on Saturday. Just get the basics — crate, bowls, collar, leash, bed, toys — and you can add more personalized stuff once you know the dog. You've got a solid plan and the right mindset. The excitement you're feeling is the right energy. Go make that landlord call, browse those rescues online to get an idea of what's available, and then show up Saturday ready to meet some dogs. I have a good feeling about this whole thing. You're going to do great. Come back after Saturday and let me know how it went, what dogs caught your eye, and whether you need help deciding between any candidates. I'm here for all the follow-up stuff too!
